repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
This PR implements a channel-based asynchronous API for Lua, replacing and extending the earlier `vim._async` prototype with a cleaner coroutine model.

## Primitives

### vim.async.spawn(func, ...)

Spawns a detached coroutine. The coroutine is tracked so yielding across resume boundaries is safe — unlike raw coroutine.resume, yielding inside a spawned coroutine will never cross a C call boundary.

### vim.async.chan(max?, timeout?)

Creates a buffered communication channel (class vim.async.Chan):
- `chan:send(...)` — pushes a message into the buffer. If the buffer is full and the caller is in a spawned coroutine, it yields until space is available. In a non-spawned context, it errors immediately.
- `chan:recv()` — pops a message from the buffer. If empty and in a spawned coroutine, it yields. Otherwise it polls with vim.wait() and errors on timeout.
- `chan:close()` — wakes all blocked senders/receivers. Senders error; receivers drain remaining messages then return nil.
- `chan:is_closed()` — check if the channel is closed.

Channels are iterable: for msg in chan do ... end consumes messages until close.

### vim.async.await(func, ...)

Invokes a callback-style async function and returns its results synchronously. Appends a synthetic callback, calls func, then yields (in a spawned coroutine) or uses vim.wait (otherwise) until the callback fires. Useful for wrapping callback-based APIs.

### vim.async.spawn_wrap(func)

Wraps a function so each call runs it in a spawned coroutine. Useful for registering non-blocking event handlers.

### vim.async.timeout

Configurable default receive timeout (default 10000ms). Pass timeout to vim.async.chan() to override per-channel.

## Integration with existing async APIs

The channel model integrates naturally with Neovim's existing async conventions. The current API supports both continuation-passing style (callbacks) and blocking style calls — adding channels internally preserves the old API while gaining context-switching capabilities. For example, `vim.async.await` bridges callback-style functions into the coroutine world, and channels can be passed to callback-based producers (as shown in the timer counter example in the docs). This makes it straightforward to layer channels into existing async subsystems without breaking compatibility.

## Comparison to vim._async

The earlier vim._async (introduced in cf0f90fe14) used a resume helper that threaded a callback through each coroutine yield — essentially a manual continuation-passing style. It required passing explicit argc indices to M.await and had no buffered communication primitive.

This PR replaces that with:
- Channel model — familiar CSP-style buffered channels with backpressure, similar to Go or Clojure's core.async
- No callback threading — spawn runs a function in a coroutine; yield/resume is handled internally
- No argc — chan:send(...) accepts any number of values, chan:recv() returns them
- Self-iteration — callable Chan objects can be used directly in for loops
- Per-channel timeout override

comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,430 @@
+--- @brief
+---
+--- `vim.async` provides primitives for coroutine-based asynchronous
+--- programming in Lua. It includes:
+---
+--- - `RingQueue` and `ListQueue`: bounded and unbounded first-in-first-out
+---   queue data structures with `push`/`pop` operations.
+--- - `spawn()`: run a function in a detached coroutine that is tracked so
+---   yielding across resume boundaries is safe.
+--- - `spawn_wrap()`: wrap a function so it runs in a spawned coroutine
+---   each time it is called.
+--- - `chan()`: create a communication channel with a bounded buffer for
+---   passing messages between coroutines, with support for backpressure
+---   and graceful close.
+--- - `await()`: invoke a callback-style async function and synchronously
+---   return its results, either by yielding (in a spawned coroutine) or
+---   via `vim.wait` (in other contexts).
+---
+local function class(body)
+  return setmetatable(body or {}, {
+    __call = function(cls, ...)
+      local object = setmetatable({}, {
+        __index = cls,
+        __call = function(obj, ...) return obj:__call(...) end
+      })
+      if object.__init then object:__init(...) end
+      return object
+    end
+  })
+end
+
+---@class vim.async.RingQueue<T>
+---@field _capacity number
+---@field _size number
+---@field _idx number
+---@field _items T[]
+---@overload fun(capacity: number): vim.async.RingQueue<T>
+local RingQueue = class {}
+
+---@param capacity number
+function RingQueue:__init(capacity)

comment_data.body: Ringbuf overrides existing values when it's full. RingQueue rejects pushing when it is full. I thought it should easier to implement a custom type with size and capacity instead of wrapping Ringbuf and calculating size based on 2 indices, since we cannot really know if ringbuf is empty or just overflowed if we don't make push() wrapper.

comment_data.body: could we enhance `vim.ringbuf` instead? it surely doesn't make sense to introduce multiple new container types

comment_regular_data.body: This looks AI-generated and doesn't look like it draws from any of the discussion in the [tracking issue](https://www.github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/19624) nor from https://github.com/lewis6991/async.nvim/ (which is referenced from the tracking issue)

I originally pulled it from my config, that's why the first commit is big. AI used mostly for documentation and some refactoring. I also really tried to find a discussion regarding asynchronous api, but unfortunately couldn't. I would appreciate
if you point why this PR does not meet project's standards and perhaps corresponding discussion. I tried to keep the implementation simple, but flexible enough. Thanks for your time.

@justinmk sorry for bothering you, just thought you may have not seen my previous message.
This particular feature gets reimplemented by every plugin author, and I understand that everybody has a different opinion on how you should do concurrent programming. If my take on it does not align with your vision, I would appreciate any real feedback. In case there is no interest in it, please let me know directly.

well, i pointed to the current "reference implementation" above, which also has issues where we are discussing various tradeoffs.

and there is a `async` label: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20state%3Aopen%20label%3Aasync

and that label includes the main issue which is https://www.github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/19624 (do not add comments there, we can discuss here if you want to)

> * Channel model — familiar CSP-style buffered channels with backpressure, similar to Go or Clojure's core.async

CSP is definitely what we favor, and is what https://github.com/lewis6991/async.nvim/ provides.


> * No callback threading — spawn runs a function in a coroutine; yield/resume is handled internally

"No callback threading" is good, but we can't only depend on coroutines, because they aren't always compatible with various use-cases. Thus https://github.com/lewis6991/async.nvim/ , and as discussed in the tracking issue, also supports "tasks" which are just plain functions.

> * No argc — chan:send(...) accepts any number of values, chan:recv() returns them

cool

> * Self-iteration — callable Chan objects can be used directly in for loops

cool

> * Per-channel timeout override

cool. what about cancel? I assume `close()` provides that

> > * Per-channel timeout override
> 
> cool. what about cancel? I assume `close()` provides that

`close()` sets _closed attribute to true and awakes all sender and receiver threads. Then if there are any messages remaining, receivers read them and on next loop iteration nil returned (since it follows lua iterator convention). If you try to send message to a closed channel, it simply errors. If you call recv() in non-spawn context, we `vim.wait()` until there are any messages or until channel is closed.

> > * No callback threading — spawn runs a function in a coroutine; yield/resume is handled internally
> 
> "No callback threading" is good, but we can't only depend on coroutines, because they aren't always compatible with various use-cases. Thus https://github.com/lewis6991/async.nvim/ , and as discussed in the tracking issue, also supports "tasks" which are just plain functions.

Essentially `spawn()` provides a safe way for spawning coroutines, and channels provide a reliable way for controlling and switching between them. I believe with these two core primitives, we should be able to build any higher-level abstractions relatively easily, including tasks if we need them.

Here is a draft:

```lua
local tasks = setmetatable({}, { __mode = 'k' })
local Task = class {}

function Task:__init(func, ...)
  self._chan = vim.async.chan()
  self._children = {}

  local parent = coroutine.running()

  vim.async.spawn(function(...)
    local coro = coroutine.running()
    tasks[coro] = self

    if tasks[parent] then
      tasks[parent]:_add_child(coro)
    end

    local ok, err = pcall(func, ...)

    self._chan:close()
    self:wait()

    if tasks[parent] then
      tasks[parent]:_remove_child(coro)
    end
    tasks[coro] = nil

    if not ok then error(err) end
  end, ...)
end

function Task:wait()
  self._chan:recv()
  for _, child in ipairs(self._children) do
    tasks[child]:wait()
  end
end

function Task:cancel()
  for _, child in ipairs(self._children) do
    tasks[child]:cancel()
  end
  self._chan:close()
end
```

Although I would prefer to keep things simple by using raw `spawn()` and channels.

Having lower-level primitives can also be useful for implementing other features, e.g. generators:

```lua
local function generator(func)
  return function(...)
    local chan = vim.async.chan()
    vim.async.spawn(function(...)
      func(function(...) return chan:send(...) end, ...)
      chan:close()
    end, ...)
    return chan
  end
end

local seq = generator(function(yield, n)
  for i = 1, n do yield(i) end
end)

for i in seq(10) do print(i) end
```

title: Async API
